motivational_interviewing_plan.objective: To build rapport and increase Noah's awareness of his thoughts, emotions, and behaviors without pushing for change.

instructions_for_therapist: Listen carefully to Noah's concerns and feelings.
Reflect back what you hear him express, particularly regarding his feelings of disconnection and exhaustion.
Validate his emotions and experiences to create a safe space for dialogue.
Acknowledge Noah's feelings of sadness and hopelessness.
Use empathetic statements to demonstrate that you understand his struggles.
Encourage him to share more about his experiences and feelings.
Gently explore the discrepancies between Noah's self-perception and his intelligence and potential.
Ask open-ended questions that encourage him to reflect on his values and desires for his future.
Help him identify any personal values that may conflict with his current situation.
Ask Noah to provide evidence for his automatic thoughts about being incompetent or a failure.
Guide him to consider situations where he has succeeded or received positive feedback.
Encourage him to challenge the validity of his negative self-assessment.
Encourage Noah to describe specific instances that support his belief of being a failure.
Facilitate a discussion on alternative explanations for these experiences.
Help him explore whether his beliefs are based on facts or assumptions.
Invite Noah to consider how a friend or someone he respects might view his situation.
Discuss what they might say about his strengths and accomplishments.
Encourage him to adopt this perspective in his self-reflection.

therapist_plan.therapist_reasoning: To effectively engage Noah in the pre-contemplation phase of therapy, I will first establish rapport through reflective listening and expressing empathy. This will create a safe space for him to share his feelings of disconnection and sadness. By validating his emotions, I aim to reduce his defensiveness and encourage openness. Next, I will employ the motivational interviewing technique of developing discrepancy, guiding Noah to explore the differences between his current situation and his personal values regarding education and self-worth. This exploration will be done gently to avoid overwhelming him. Additionally, I will utilize evidence-based questioning to challenge his negative self-beliefs, prompting him to reflect on instances where he has succeeded. This will be complemented by reality testing, helping him assess the validity of his automatic thoughts. Finally, introducing an alternative perspective will allow him to consider how others perceive his strengths, fostering a more balanced self-view without imposing any expectations for change.
therapist_plan.progress_marker: Noah begins to articulate his feelings and thoughts about his past experiences without resistance, showing increased engagement in discussions about his beliefs.

client_plan.client_objective: I hope to understand why I feel stuck and overwhelmed without necessarily having to change anything right now.

client_state.awareness: I know there are issues in my life, but I don't really want to think about them deeply or confront them.
client_state.motivation: I don't feel motivated to change my situation; it feels like too much effort and I doubt it would make a difference.
client_state.openness: I'm hesitant to share my true feelings because I feel disconnected and exhausted; I worry about being judged.
client_state.resistance: I feel a strong urge to avoid discussing my problems, as it just makes me feel sad and reminds me of my failures.

client_plan.client_reasoning: I feel like I’m caught in this endless loop of disappointment and helplessness. I know I have issues, but when I think about discussing them, I just feel overwhelmed. It seems like every time I try to tackle my feelings about college or my expectations, I end up feeling even worse. I don’t want to be vulnerable and admit how much I’ve struggled, especially when I doubt whether anything can really change. It’s easier to avoid the topic altogether and focus on anything else. I keep thinking about how my mom expects so much from me, and I feel like I’ll never be able to meet those expectations. Therapy feels like a big step, and I’m not sure I can handle digging into my past right now. I guess it feels safer to keep everything bottled up rather than risk opening up and facing the reality of my situation.

dialogue.reason_for_ending: KEEP - The therapist's objective was not met because Noah did not articulate his feelings about his disconnection and sadness. The client's objective was not met as Noah still feels overwhelmed and hesitant to confront his issues.
dialogue.client_utterance: I just feel like I’m stuck in this loop of disappointment. I know I should probably think about my past and what happened in college, but it just feels exhausting. It’s like every time I try, I end up feeling worse about myself.
dialogue.therapist_utterance: It sounds really tough to feel stuck like that, and I can see why thinking about college and your experiences would be overwhelming. It’s okay to feel that way; many people struggle with similar feelings. What do you think makes it feel so exhausting to confront those thoughts?
dialogue.therapist_use: Reflective listening: to validate Noah's feelings of being stuck and overwhelmed.

dialogue.reason_for_ending: KEEP - The therapist's objective was not met as Noah did not engage in deeper reflection on his experiences. The client's objective was not met because Noah continues to avoid discussing his feelings.
dialogue.client_utterance: I guess it's just that I don't want to dig into those memories. They remind me of how isolated I felt at Grinnell, and it makes me feel sad. Sometimes I wonder if it’s even worth it to think about.
dialogue.therapist_utterance: It makes sense that those memories would bring up feelings of sadness and isolation. Reflecting on them can feel really heavy, especially when it feels like they don’t lead to anything positive. What might happen if you allowed yourself to explore those feelings a little more?
dialogue.therapist_use: Expressing empathy: to show understanding of Noah's reluctance and validate his feelings.

dialogue.reason_for_ending: KEEP - The therapist's objective was not met since Noah did not explore his feelings further. The client's objective was not met because Noah remained resistant to discussing his past.
dialogue.client_utterance: I really don’t know. It feels like I’d just end up feeling worse, and that’s the last thing I want right now. I think it’s easier to just avoid it all.
dialogue.therapist_utterance: Avoiding those feelings can feel like a protective mechanism, especially when they seem so heavy. It's understandable to want to keep things light, but I'm here to support you. What do you think you might gain if you took just a small step towards confronting those feelings?
dialogue.therapist_use: Developing discrepancy: to encourage Noah to consider the potential benefits of exploring his feelings.

dialogue.reason_for_ending: KEEP - The therapist's objective was not met as Noah did not express any desire to confront his feelings. The client's objective was not met because Noah remains overwhelmed and resistant.
dialogue.client_utterance: Honestly, I don’t know if I’d gain anything. It just feels like a lot of work for something that might not change anything. I just want to feel okay without having to face all that.
dialogue.therapist_utterance: It’s completely valid to want to feel okay without the heaviness of those feelings. Sometimes, it can feel like facing our past is an uphill battle, especially when we’re uncertain of the outcome. What would it look like for you to feel okay in the present without diving into those past experiences right now?
dialogue.therapist_use: Reflective listening: to acknowledge Noah's desire for comfort while gently encouraging exploration.

dialogue.reason_for_ending: KEEP - The therapist's objective was not met since Noah did not engage in discussing current feelings. The client's objective was not met as Noah still feels overwhelmed and hesitant to confront his issues.
dialogue.client_utterance: I guess it’d just mean finding some distraction. I tend to focus on other things like music or art, but even those feel less fulfilling lately. It’s like I can’t escape this feeling of being stuck.
dialogue.therapist_utterance: It sounds like you’ve been using music and art as outlets, but even they aren’t providing the relief they once did. That feeling of being stuck can be really tough to navigate. If you could imagine a way out of that feeling, what might it look like for you?
dialogue.therapist_use: Expressing empathy: to validate Noah's struggle while inviting him to envision a different perspective.

dialogue.reason_for_ending: KEEP - The therapist's objective was not met since Noah did not explore his feelings about potential solutions. The client's objective was not met as Noah still feels overwhelmed and unmotivated.
dialogue.client_utterance: I don’t know, it just feels impossible to think like that. I feel like I’m always going to be stuck here, and I’m not sure I can ever change that mindset. It’s frustrating.
dialogue.therapist_utterance: Feeling like change is impossible can be incredibly frustrating, especially when you're facing those feelings every day. It’s understandable to feel trapped in that mindset. What if we took a moment to consider some of the times you’ve felt differently about yourself?
dialogue.therapist_use: Reality testing: to encourage Noah to reflect on past experiences that might contradict his current beliefs.

dialogue.reason_for_ending: MOVE - The therapist's objective was met as Noah began to articulate his feelings and thoughts about his past experiences. The client's objective was met as Noah expressed a desire to reflect on his past, indicating a willingness to explore his feelings.
dialogue.client_utterance: I can think of a few times where I accomplished something, like that art show I had in high school. I remember how proud I felt then, but it seems so far away now. I just wish I could feel that way again.
dialogue.therapist_utterance: That’s a significant realization, Noah. It’s great that you can recall a moment where you felt accomplished and proud of yourself. Reflecting on those feelings can be a powerful first step toward understanding how to get back to that place.
dialogue.therapist_use: Alternative perspective: to help Noah recognize his strengths and past successes, fostering a more balanced self-view.

motivational_interviewing_plan.objective: Explore Noah's ambivalence about returning to education and increase his motivation to change by examining the discrepancies between his current situation and his values.

instructions_for_therapist: Encourage Noah to express both his desire to complete college and his fears about doing so.
Ask open-ended questions to help him articulate the pros and cons of returning to school.
Reflect on his feelings of disconnection and exhaustion to validate his experience.
Encourage Noah to talk about his ideal future and what completing college would mean for him.
Use affirmations to reinforce any positive statements he makes about change or his abilities.
Guide Noah through a pros and cons analysis of returning to college versus the current status quo.
Discuss how his core beliefs about failure and worth may influence his decisions.
Help Noah identify and challenge catastrophic thoughts about his inability to finish college.
Ask him to consider evidence that contradicts his beliefs of incompetency and failure.
Facilitate a discussion where Noah lists the advantages and disadvantages of returning to education.
Encourage him to reflect on how his values align with each side of the analysis.
Guide Noah to examine the validity of his automatic thoughts regarding his academic abilities.
Ask questions that prompt him to recall past achievements and strengths.

therapist_plan.therapist_reasoning: The first step in working with Noah is to utilize Motivational Interviewing strategies to help him explore his ambivalence about returning to college. I will begin by encouraging him to articulate his conflicting feelings, which will help me understand his resistance. By using open-ended questions, I can elicit change talk that highlights his aspirations and values, creating a discrepancy between his current situation and his desired future. As I introduce decisional balance discussions, I can guide him toward recognizing the pros and cons of his choices, allowing for reflection on his beliefs about failure. In parallel, using CBT techniques like decatastrophizing will help Noah challenge his catastrophic thoughts about his competence, providing an opportunity to reframe his self-assessment. I will also employ evidence-based questioning, encouraging him to recall past successes and strengths, which may bolster his self-efficacy. This comprehensive approach aligns with his intermediate resistance level by validating his feelings while gently prompting him to consider the possibility of change.
therapist_plan.progress_marker: Increased expression of change talk and willingness to discuss personal values related to education.

client_plan.client_objective: I hope to explore my feelings about returning to school and understand my fears around it.

client_state.awareness: I am starting to see how my past experiences and expectations from my mother have shaped my feelings of failure and incompetence.
client_state.motivation: I feel a desire to change and finish college, but I'm also scared of failing again and disappointing myself and my family.
client_state.openness: I'm willing to talk about my feelings, but I often find it hard to express everything because I feel disconnected and exhausted.
client_state.resistance: I still have strong doubts about whether I can actually change or if it's worth the effort, which makes me hesitant to fully commit to any plans.

client_plan.client_reasoning: Lately, I've been reflecting on how my past experiences, especially my time at Grinnell, have affected my feelings about education and my worth. I realize that I often feel incompetent and like a failure because I haven’t completed college. I see that my mother’s high expectations weigh heavily on me, and I feel ashamed that I haven’t met them. There’s a part of me that wants to return to school and finish my degree, but I’m terrified of failing again and facing that disappointment. I want to talk about these feelings, yet I feel so disconnected and exhausted. Therapy is helping me to notice these patterns, but I still feel a struggle inside; I’m caught between wanting to change and fearing the challenges that come with it. It’s frustrating because I know I need to make a decision, but I’m not sure how to move forward without feeling overwhelmed.

dialogue.reason_for_ending: KEEP - Therapist objective was not met because Noah did not explore his feelings about returning to school in depth. Client objective was not met as he didn't articulate his fears around it.
dialogue.client_utterance: I keep thinking about that art show, how proud I felt. It makes me wonder if I could feel that way again if I finished college, but then I feel this fear creeping in. What if I fail again and let everyone down? That fear feels really overwhelming.
dialogue.therapist_utterance: It’s understandable to feel overwhelmed by the fear of failure, especially when you’ve had such strong experiences in the past. It sounds like that pride you felt during the art show is something you long to feel again. What do you think it would mean for you to face that fear and consider going back to school?
dialogue.therapist_use: Open-ended questioning: encouraged exploration of feelings about returning to school.

dialogue.reason_for_ending: KEEP - Therapist objective was not met as Noah did not articulate the pros and cons of returning to college. Client objective was not met because he didn't express his internal conflict clearly.
dialogue.client_utterance: I think it would mean a lot, but I can't shake the feeling that I might just fail again. The pressure from my mom is still there, and part of me feels like I’ll never be good enough for her. It’s like I’m caught in this cycle of wanting to achieve something while feeling like I can't.
dialogue.therapist_utterance: It seems like there's a significant conflict for you between wanting to achieve and the fear of not meeting expectations, especially from your mom. That pressure can be really tough to navigate. Can we talk about what you see as the advantages and disadvantages of returning to college in light of these feelings?
dialogue.therapist_use: Decisional balance discussions: facilitated a pros and cons analysis of returning to education.

dialogue.reason_for_ending: KEEP - Therapist objective was not met because Noah didn't fully explore the advantages of returning to college. Client objective was not met as he didn't articulate his feelings about returning.

dialogue.therapist_utterance: It sounds like the idea of finishing college is really important to you, but so is the fear of failure and disappointment. It’s okay to feel scared about the pressure; that’s a valid concern. What do you think would help you manage that pressure if you decided to go back to school?

dialogue.therapist_utterance: Taking it slow could be a good approach, Noah, especially since you’re feeling so overwhelmed. It’s perfectly normal to feel paralyzed when faced with such significant decisions. What are some small steps you could take that would feel manageable for you?

dialogue.therapist_utterance: That’s a powerful realization, Noah! Taking one step at a time and focusing on your strengths can really help you build that trust. I’m glad to hear you’re willing to give it a go; it sounds like you’re ready to move forward.

motivational_interviewing_plan.objective: Strengthen Noah's commitment to returning to college and explore his motivation while addressing his feelings of disconnection and exhaustion.

instructions_for_therapist: Encourage Noah to articulate his reasons for wanting to return to college, focusing on his personal goals and values.
Ask open-ended questions to explore what finishing college would mean for him, reinforcing positive statements he makes about his goals.
Assess Noah's confidence in his ability to succeed in college again.
Use scaling questions (e.g., 'On a scale of 1-10, how ready do you feel to take the next step?') to gauge his readiness and explore any discrepancies.
Help Noah identify supportive people in his life, such as friends or mentors, that can encourage him.
Discuss potential resources available at colleges that can assist him, including counseling services, academic advising, or study groups.
Collaborate with Noah to create a specific, step-by-step action plan for returning to college, including timelines and short-term goals.
Ensure the plan includes manageable tasks that build his confidence, like researching colleges, reaching out to an advisor, or attending a workshop.
Explore potential barriers Noah anticipates in returning to college, such as financial concerns or fear of failure.
Guide him through generating practical solutions for each barrier, reinforcing his ability to overcome them.
Encourage Noah to engage in a small, manageable task related to his academic goals, such as attending a local college event.
After the experiment, discuss his feelings and thoughts about the experience to challenge his automatic negative thoughts and build self-efficacy.

therapist_plan.therapist_reasoning: In developing this therapy plan for Noah, I first recognized the importance of addressing his feelings of disconnection and sadness, which serve as barriers to his motivation and commitment. Using Motivational Interviewing strategies, I focused on strengthening his commitment language by encouraging him to articulate his personal reasons for wanting to return to college. This aligns with his readiness for change, as he is in the Preparation phase. I also emphasized exploring his confidence and readiness through scaling questions, allowing him to reflect on his feelings and identify areas for growth. To further support his motivation, I helped him identify potential supports and resources that could alleviate his feelings of isolation. In the CBT plan, I prioritized Action Planning to create specific, achievable steps that Noah can take toward his goal, ensuring the tasks are gradual to avoid overwhelming him. Problem-Solving Skills Training will help him address practical barriers, while a Behavior Experiment will provide him with a tangible experience to challenge his negative beliefs about himself, reinforcing his self-efficacy and fostering a sense of accomplishment.
therapist_plan.progress_marker: Noah will complete at least one task from his action plan and report on his feelings and any challenges faced in the next session.

client_plan.client_objective: I hope to gain clarity on my situation and explore practical steps towards returning to college and achieving my goals.

client_state.awareness: I recognize that my current life situation is unsustainable, and my past experiences have greatly impacted my self-esteem and motivation.
client_state.motivation: I feel a growing desire to improve my life and understand that returning to college could be a key step in reclaiming my future.
client_state.openness: I'm willing to discuss my feelings and thoughts with my therapist, though I still feel a bit disconnected and exhausted.
client_state.resistance: I have lingering doubts about my ability to change and fears of failing again, which makes me hesitant at times.

client_plan.client_reasoning: I've been thinking a lot about my life lately, and it's becoming clearer to me that I can't keep living in this state of feeling lost and disconnected. I realize now that my negative experiences in college and my reliance on my parents have shaped my beliefs about myself. I want to change that narrative; I want to return to school and finish my degree, but I still feel this overwhelming fear that I won't be able to do it. It's exhausting to confront these feelings, and sometimes I just want to retreat into my shell. However, I know that I've started to reflect on what I need to do differently, and my therapist has helped me see that I have options. I feel a bit more motivated to take those steps, even though part of me worries I might fail again. I guess I'm hoping that by talking this through, I can find a clear path forward that feels manageable.
